title: Reduction Distal Radius Volar Plate
must_know: Distal radius responsible for 80% of axial load through wrist. Articulates with scaphoid via scaphoid fossa, lunate via lunate fossa, distal ulna via ulnar/sigmoid notch. Comprised of three columns: radial (radial styloid/scaphoid fossa; attachments for brachioradialis, long radiolunate, radioscapho

Lister's tubercle on dorsal surface ~1 cm proximal to articular surface; EPL tendon lies just ulnar to it before sharp turn into thumb. Important landmark to avoid EPL attritional rupture with long volar plate screws.

Watershed line: most volar and prominent aspect of distal radius (lunate portion most prominent with short radiolunate ligament attachment). Critical region to remain proximal to for volar plate fixation to avoid FPL rupture.
Internervous plane in FCR approach between FCR (median n.) and FPL (AIN).

Volar FCR approach: incision along FCR tendon sheath (ulnar/radial curve at crease), develop plane, retract FCR ulnarly, incise FCR sheath dorsally, retract FPL ulnarly, incise PQ borders and elevate. Proximal/distal extensions as needed for median n. or scaphoid.
Dorsal approach useful for displaced intra-articular dorsal lip fx or carpal work; no true internervous plane, between 3rd/4th extensor compartments.
Volar plating preferred; plate must not be placed distal to watershed line to avoid FPL irritation/rupture. Assess screw penetration with skyline and 23° elevated lateral views.

structures_at_risk.structure: Palmar cutaneous branch of median nerve

common_pimp_questions: What is the watershed line of the distal radius and why must volar plates remain proximal to it?
